Revealing the similarities and differences in elasticity and structural dynamics between double-stranded RNA and double-stranded DNA and their physical mechanisms

Summary: The applicant discovered the “ion-clamp” effect in the major groove of RNA; elucidated that environmental factors regulate the helical number of DNA and RNA through the diameter and the major groove, respectively; and revealed that monovalent salts unexpectedly cause charge inversion of DNA and RNA.

One key difference between DNA and RNA is that multivalent cations condense DNA but have difficulty condensing RNA, and the mechanism remains unclear. We found that multivalent cations embed into the major groove of double-stranded RNA to form an “ion clamp”, which stiffens RNA and therefore makes RNA difficult to condense (Figure 1A, PRL 2020, last corresponding author), and high concentrations of anions can disrupt the RNA ion clamp (Commun Biol 2026, last corresponding author). The applicant discovered the “ion-clamp” effect of RNA and elucidated the mechanism by which multivalent cations cannot condense RNA, explaining from the perspective of genome compaction why DNA, rather than RNA, became the carrier of genetic material.




Figure 1. Key differences between double-stranded DNA and double-stranded RNA. (A) Unlike DNA, multivalent cations embed into the major groove of RNA to form an “ion clamp”, preventing RNA condensation (PRL 2020, last corresponding author). (B) DNA responds to the environment by changing its diameter (Sci Adv 2022); therefore, entropic stretching force increases the diameter and unwinds DNA (Nat Commun 2025_2). RNA responds to the environment by changing the major groove (PNAS 2023), whereas the “ion clamp” forces RNA to respond to the environment by changing its diameter (PRL 2022).

When we apply stretching force, DNA overwinds whereas RNA unwinds, and the mechanism remains unclear. We revealed that environmental factors, including ions, temperature, stretching force, and protein binding, regulate the helical density of DNA by changing its diameter (Sci Adv 2022, co-corresponding author). Thus, after multivalent ions bind to DNA, entropic stretching force increases the DNA diameter and thereby unwinds DNA (Nat Commun 2025_2, co-corresponding author). In contrast, environmental factors regulate the helical density of RNA by changing the major-groove width (PNAS 2023, co-corresponding author), explaining the opposite torsional responses of RNA and DNA. Multivalent cations embed into the major groove of RNA to form an “ion clamp”, which suppresses major-groove deformation and forces environmental factors to regulate helical density by changing the diameter (PRL 2022, co-corresponding author). This unified model quantitatively elucidates how environmental factors, including ions, temperature, stretching force, and protein binding, regulate the helical density of double-stranded DNA and double-stranded RNA (Figure 1B). The bending rigidity of RNA is significantly higher than that of DNA (Biophys J 2019, sole corresponding author, recognized by the journal as the best paper of 2019), but its mechanism was unclear. We found that, for both double-stranded RNA and double-stranded DNA, bending rigidity depends on the total groove width, that is, the sum of the major-groove width and minor-groove width, thereby clarifying the origin of the bending rigidity of double-stranded DNA and double-stranded RNA (Nucleic Acids Res 2024, co-corresponding author).

Double-stranded DNA and double-stranded RNA both carry a large number of negative charges. The traditional view is that increasing the concentration of monovalent cations, such as Na+ and K+, neutralizes the negative charges of duplexes and reduces the repulsion between the two strands, thereby increasing duplex stability. However, we found that, at high concentrations, monovalent cations unexpectedly cause double-stranded RNA and DNA to undergo charge inversion, making them positively charged and reducing duplex stability. Combining experimental data with theoretical calculations, we constructed a charge-inversion phase diagram as a function of cation valence and concentration (Figure 2, Nat Commun 2025_1, last corresponding author, representative work). High concentrations of monovalent cations can also cause single-stranded DNA to undergo charge inversion (Macromolecules 2025, last corresponding author). This work challenges the traditional view that “monovalent cations can only weaken the electronegativity of double-stranded DNA, whereas only multivalent cations can cause DNA charge inversion”, provides important insights for further studying the mechanism of intermolecular attraction/repulsion between biomacromolecules, and promotes the development of condensed-matter physics and polyelectrolyte theory.




Figure 2. Cation-induced charge inversion of DNA and RNA (Nat Commun 2025_1, last corresponding author). (A) High concentrations of monovalent salts reduce the stability of double-stranded DNA and double-stranded RNA. (B) Phase diagram of DNA charge inversion as a function of ion valence and concentration.
